narrative: Supply chains break because every exception creates a real-time coordination problem across carriers, suppliers, warehouses, inventory systems, customer commitments, and cost tradeoffs. Most logistics teams already have alerts, but alerts are not resolution. The value is in giving the control tower an autonomous response layer. An agent can detect an exception, gather the relevant operating context, compare possible responses, trigger narrow approved actions, and escalate only the cases that truly require a planner or operations lead.
buyerPromise: We turn exception management from alert triage into guided autonomous resolution, so planners spend time on true tradeoffs instead of repetitive investigation.

subCriteria.name: Event-driven orchestration
subCriteria.rationale: Real-time exception detection and response staging

subCriteria.name: Scenario comparison
subCriteria.rationale: Cost/SLA tradeoff analysis across multiple options

subCriteria.name: TMS/WMS integration
subCriteria.rationale: Core logistics systems have established APIs

subCriteria.name: Carrier feeds
subCriteria.rationale: Telematics, EDI, and API feeds well-structured

subCriteria.name: Cost data
subCriteria.rationale: Claims, penalty, and expedite costs traceable

subCriteria.name: Event streams
subCriteria.rationale: Carrier event stream reliability varies significantly

subCriteria.name: Narrow write authority
subCriteria.rationale: Autonomous actions bounded by clear economic rules

subCriteria.name: Planner override
subCriteria.rationale: Human planners can override or correct agent actions

subCriteria.name: Alert fatigue
subCriteria.rationale: Too many false alerts erode trust in the system

examples.name: Suntory Global Spirits
examples.source: project44 case study on Suntory Global Spirits
examples.challenge: Pandemic disruption drove stockouts, detention/demurrage, expedites, and manual exception handling across thousands of SKUs.
examples.scale: Logistics costs rose from 6% to 25% of goods cost.
examples.humanHoursAndDollarImpact: 99% reduction in demurrage costs. Direct labor-hour savings not disclosed but siloed data and manual processes cited as root causes.
examples.whyItMatters: Exception management becomes existential when disruptions swing logistics cost by that much.

examples.name: Georgia-Pacific
examples.challenge: Needed to handle multimodal visibility, yard operations, customer service workload, and delay coordination more efficiently.
examples.scale: Multimodal logistics and yard management complexity across truckload, ocean, and yards.
examples.humanHoursAndDollarImpact: Manual processes reduced by 80%. Customer-service workload decreased by 87%. Detention and demurrage fees also fell.
examples.whyItMatters: Strongest evidence that exception handling is not a monitoring problem — it is a coordination problem.

examples.name: Hogland Transfer Company
examples.source: Samsara case study on Hogland Transfer
examples.challenge: Time-sensitive logistics provider needed better visibility into incidents, dispatching, and claims defense.
examples.scale: Supports just-in-time delivery for manufacturers and aerospace customers.
examples.humanHoursAndDollarImpact: Investigation time fell by 52%. Up to 4 hours saved per incident. 8 hours per week freed. One false claim disproven quickly, avoiding ~$80,000 in costs.
examples.whyItMatters: Even framed as operations visibility, the real value comes from reducing the human cost of investigating and coordinating exceptions.

quickStart: One exception class, one team, one operating surface
avoid: 'AI control tower for the whole network'
Broad optimization across every node on day one
Autonomous action without clear economic rules
consultantSignals: Gartner: By 2030, 50% of cross-functional supply-chain solutions will use intelligent agents to autonomously execute decisions. Start where data is accessible, behavior is verifiable, and ROI is high.
BCG: Cost and resilience are inseparable — US OEM margins could fall 5-7 percentage points (up to 75%) due to tariff and supply-chain exposure.
Deloitte: 64% of business leaders feel urgency to adopt gen AI in supply chain. Emphasizes resilience, response speed, and interconnected use cases.
obstacles: Too many false alerts and low-confidence predictions
Unclear ownership between logistics, customer service, procurement, and supply planning
Fragmented SOPs by site or region
whereDataLives: Customer-service tickets related to delays and misses
openQuestions: Which exception class is both frequent and expensive enough to anchor the first sale?
Which teams own response today and where do handoffs break?
What is the real cost of a missed appointment, expedite, or dwell hour?
Which actions can be taken autonomously versus recommended?
How reliable is the event stream from carriers and sites?
How will planners override or correct agent actions without losing trust?
